what_it_does: Defines the SUBJECT face as a semantic model for how artifacts project a shared subject through sectors, cuts, routes, sibling consistency, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage.
observations: Meta frames the file as the semantic side of the system and distinguishes subject sectors from artifact channels.
Subject space is described as subjective, continuous, bounded, and soft-edged, contrasted with sharp artifact space.
Sectors are radial divisions around an origin and become linear only when a telling orders them.
Projection is modeled as authored cut plus route, with cuts guided by changes in information density.
Sibling artifacts are expected to agree on figures, terms, and claims across the same subject.
Lexicon scope is assigned to subject space rather than to individual artifacts.
Canvas geography treats slides as places on a subject map and presentations as camera tours.
Coverage maps connect artifact addresses to subject sectors for gap and overlap analysis.
standouts: The cut heuristic links seams to natural content differences rather than distance or count.
The file explicitly separates soft semantic territory from sharp structural partitions.
It treats multiple artifacts as sibling projections over one shared subject space.
conventions: Uses declarative JSON objects with prose fields rather than code symbols or executable structure.
Includes status, evidence, cross_ref, verdict, and invariant fields as local annotation patterns.
Uses emphatic capitalization for model terms such as SUBJECT, SEMANTIC, CUT, ROUTE, and COVERAGE MAP.
Stores predictions and confirmations together inside the same conceptual sections.
concerns: Several fields contain dense compound claims that may be hard to atomize without additional schema.
Some sections refer to external files or systems, but those references are not resolved inside this file.
The file mixes model definition, provenance, evidence, and later verdicts in one layer.
notes: Brief this as the semantic territory model: subjects have soft radial sectors, artifacts are authored projections through cuts and routes, and sibling artifacts must remain consistent.
questions: What schema should distinguish prediction, confirmation, evidence, and law fields?
How are sector boundaries represented when the file says they are real but soft?
What data structure records a coverage map from artifact addresses to subject sectors?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it defines the SUBJECT face as the semantic side and elaborates sectors, projections, sibling agreement, lexicon scope, canvas geography, and coverage.
apparent_themes: semantic territory versus structural artifact space
authored judgment in cut and route choices
coverage mapping over a shared subject
intention_signals: It appears to formalize how multiple design artifacts can be understood as different authored tours or resolutions of the same semantic subject territory.
novelty: The distinct move is treating document structure as a projection from soft subject geography into sharp artifact partitions through authored cuts and routes.
contribution: Contributes a subject-space ledger layer that keeps sector geometry, projection mechanics, sibling consistency, lexicon scope, canvas-map interpretation, and coverage analysis as neutral substrate concepts.
summary_for_embedding: subject.json defines a semantic subject-space model where radial soft-edged sectors are projected into sharp artifacts through authored cuts and routes while sibling artifacts share terms, claims, and coverage over the same subject.
intention_for_embedding: This file was for describing the SUBJECT face as the territory-side model behind artifacts, including how content is cut, routed, compared, and mapped across sibling outputs.
